message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 13
Recent changes: Added enchantmentSurcharge accumulator alongside cursedSurcharge, with 30% surcharge for enchantment >= 5

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text: I'll start the refactor phase by reading the necessary files.

content.content:
1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted inline `{ type: string }` to exported `type Item` for function signature clarity
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `items: Item[]` reads better than `items: { type: string }[]`
6	- Naming established: `Item` (type alias for item objects), `calculateBasePremium` (function)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced if/else conditional with `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table (`Record<string, number>`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- declarative map makes type-to-premium mapping explicit and treats all item types symmetrically
11	- Naming established: `BASE_PREMIUM` (module-level constant mapping item type to base premium value)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: added `Readonly` wrapper to `BASE_PREMIUM` type annotation
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- communicates immutability of configuration constant and enables compile-time mutation prevention
16	- Naming established: --
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): attempted destructuring `items[0]` into named binding but reverted -- mass increased from 8 to 15 with only marginal clarity gain; code already at local optimum after 3 prior refinement cycles
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- Change: replaced `Readonly<Record<string, number>>` with `as const` assertion and derived `ItemType = keyof typeof BASE_PREMIUM` union type
23	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `Item.type` is now a precise union instead of `string`, enabling compile-time validation of item types
24	- Naming established: `ItemType` (union type: `"sword" | "amulet" | "staff" | "potion" | "rune"`)
25	
26	## After cycle 6
27	- Change: replaced imperative `for` loop + mutable `sum` with `items.reduce(...)` single expression
28	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- reduce expresses "fold to sum" directly and eliminates 2 assignments (mass 28 -> 18)
29	- Naming established: --
30	
31	## After cycle 7
32	- Change: extracted `isComponentBlock(items)` helper, introduced `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES` named constants
33	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- inline condition `items.length === 3 && items.every(...) && BASE_PREMIUM[...] === 25` was opaque; extracted function makes block-detection logic self-documenting; mass +5 justified by clarity gain
34	- Naming established: `isComponentBlock` (predicate), `BLOCK_SIZE` (3), `BLOCK_PREMIUM` (60), `COMPONENT_TYPES` (ReadonlySet of component item types eligible for block discount)
35	
36	## After cycle 8
37	- Change: restructured `isComponentBlock` with early-return guard clause and extracted `blockType` named binding from repeated `items[0].type`
38	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- guard clause separates precondition from core logic; `blockType` names the concept more clearly than indexed access; mass +1 justified by clarity
39	- Naming established: `blockType` (local binding in `isComponentBlock` for the type that defines the block)
40	
41	## After cycle 9
42	- Change: removed dead `isComponentBlock` function (no longer called after green phase replaced it with inline group-by-type block detection in `calculateBasePremium`)
43	- Rationale: Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- dead code adds cognitive load and 11 mass units for zero value
44	- Naming established: --
45	
46	## After cycle 10
47	- Change: merged cursed surcharge loop into item iteration loop; introduced `cursedSurcharge` named accumulator separate from `total`
48	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named separation of base premiums (`total`) vs item-specific modifiers (`cursedSurcharge`) clarifies composition; reduced loops from 3 to 2
49	- Naming established: `cursedSurcharge` (accumulator for item-level cursed modifier, kept separate from base premium total)
50

content.text:
All 13 tests pass. Now let me proceed with the refactor phase systematically.

## Step 0: Architecture Memory -- Loaded

I've read `architecture-notes.md`. Key context: the code has been through 10 cycles. Recent changes (cycles 9-10) removed dead `isComponentBlock`, merged cursed surcharge into the main loop, and the latest green phase added `enchantmentSurcharge` accumulator.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

**Naming Evaluation**:
- Current name: `calculateBasePremium`
- Function purpose based on all 13 tests: calculates sum of base premiums (with block discount for component types), plus item-specific surcharges (cursed 50%, enchantment >= 5 at 30%)
- Question: Does "calculateBasePremium" clearly reveal this intent?
- Assessment: The function now computes more than just "base premium" -- it also adds item-specific surcharges (cursed surcharge, enchantment surcharge). The name "calculateBasePremium" suggests only the base premium portion.
- However, looking at the test file structure, the surcharge tests are under "Item-specific premium modifiers" which is separate from "Policy-wide premium modifiers". The function currently computes item-level premiums (base + item-specific modifiers). Renaming to `calculateItemPremium` or `calculatePremium` could better reflect the full scope.
- Consideration: There are still many todo tests for policy-wide modifiers, processing fees, etc. The function name may evolve further. For now, `calculateBasePremium` is misleading because it includes surcharges that are beyond "base" premium.
- Recommendation: Rename to `calculatePremium` since it computes more than just the base -- it includes item-specific modifiers. When policy-wide modifiers are added later the name still works.

Wait -- let me check the test file imports to see how it's being used.

The test imports `calculateBasePremium` and the test descriptions include "before policy modifiers and fee" for the surcharge test. So the test authors view the function as computing the pre-policy-modifier premium. The name `calculateBasePremium` actually could be read as "the base premium before policy adjustments." But it's not just the "base" -- it includes item surcharges too.

Let me think more carefully: "base premium" in insurance terminology typically means the starting premium before policy-level adjustments. Item-specific surcharges are part of computing the item-level premium that feeds into the base. So `calculateBasePremium` could be interpreted as "calculate the base premium for the policy" which includes item surcharges but not policy modifiers. This is defensible.

For now, the name is adequate. Let me look for other improvements.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Let me calculate the current mass of `calculateBasePremium`.

```
export function calculateBasePremium(items: Item[]): number {
  const groups = new Map<ItemType, number>();           // binding: groups (1), invocation: new Map() (2)
  let cursedSurcharge = 0;                              // assignment: (6), constant: 0 (1)
  let enchantmentSurcharge = 0;                         // assignment: (6), constant: 0 (1)
  for (const item of items) {                           // loop: (5), binding: item (1)
    groups.set(item.type, (groups.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1);  // invocation: set (2), invocation: get (2), constant: 0 (1), constant: 1 (1), binding: item.type x2 implicit
    if (item.cursed) {                                  // conditional: (4)
      cursedSurcharge += BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] * 0.5; // assignment: += (6), constant: 0.5 (1), invocation: BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] (2)
    }
    if (item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= 5) {  // conditional: (4), conditional: && (4), constant: undefined (1), constant: 5 (1)
      enchantmentSurcharge += BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] * 0.3;       // assignment: += (6), constant: 0.3 (1), invocation: BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] (2)
    }
  }

  let total = 0;                                        // assignment: (6), constant: 0 (1)
  for (const [type, count] of groups) {                 // loop: (5), binding: type (1), binding: count (1)
    if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === BLOCK_SIZE) {  // conditional: (4), conditional: && (4), invocation: has (2)
      total += BLOCK_PREMIUM;                           // assignment: += (6)
    } else {                                            // conditional: else (counted with if)
      total += count * BASE_PREMIUM[type];              // assignment: += (6), invocation: BASE_PREMIUM[type] (2)
    }
  }
  return total + cursedSurcharge + enchantmentSurcharge; // bindings already counted
}
```

Let me do a careful recount:

**Constants**: 0, 0, 0, 1, 0.5, undefined, 5, 0.3, 0 = 9 constants x 1 = 9
**Bindings**: items (param: 1), groups (1), cursedSurcharge (1), enchantmentSurcharge (1), item (1), type (1), count (1) = 7 x 1 = 7
**Invocations**: new Map (2), groups.set (2), groups.get (2), BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] (2), BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] (2), COMPONENT_TYPES.has (2), BASE_PREMIUM[type] (2) = 7 invocations x 2 = 14
**Conditionals**: if(item.cursed) (4), if(enchantment !== undefined) (4), && enchantment >= 5 (4), if(COMPONENT_TYPES.has && count===BLOCK_SIZE) (4), && (4) = 5 x 4 = 20
**Loops**: for(item of items) (5), for([type,count] of groups) (5) = 2 x 5 = 10
**Assignments**: let cursedSurcharge = 0 (6), let enchantmentSurcharge = 0 (6), cursedSurcharge += (6), enchantmentSurcharge += (6), let total = 0 (6), total += BLOCK_PREMIUM (6), total += count*BASE_PREMIUM (6) = 7 x 6 = 42

**Total Mass: 9 + 7 + 14 + 20 + 10 + 42 = 102**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass
All 13 tests pass. Confirmed above.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
Looking at the code, I notice:
1. The enchantment condition `item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= 5` is verbose. The `!== undefined` check is redundant because if `enchantment` is `undefined`, then `undefined >= 5` is `false`. We can simplify to just `item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= 5` -- actually, `undefined >= 5` evaluates to `false` in JavaScript, so the `!== undefined` guard is unnecessary. But it does reveal intent: it shows the developer explicitly considered the optional property case.

2. The two surcharge accumulators (`cursedSurcharge` and `enchantmentSurcharge`) are structurally parallel. They both follow the pattern: check condition on item, add percentage of base premium. This is a possible duplication candidate but extracting it now might be premature -- there are only two surcharges and extracting a generic "surcharge calculator" would add abstraction without much clarity gain.

3. The function body has grown -- two loops, three mutable accumulators, two conditional blocks inside the first loop. The function does two distinct things: (a) compute base premiums with block discount, and (b) compute item-specific surcharges. These could be separated for clarity.

Let me consider a structural refactoring: extract an `itemSurcharge` helper function that computes the surcharge for a single item. This would:
- Make the main function's loop body simpler
- Name the concept of "item surcharge" explicitly
- Separate concerns

Let me try this.

### Proposed Refactoring: Extract `itemSurcharge` helper

This separates the "compute surcharge for one item" logic from the grouping/block logic.

content.text: All 13 tests pass. The refactoring is valid. Let me verify the final state of the file.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**`itemSurcharge` function mass:**
- Constants: 0, 0.5, undefined, 5, 0.3 = 5 x 1 = 5
- Bindings: item (param: 1), base (1), surcharge (1) = 3 x 1 = 3
- Invocations: BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] (2) = 1 x 2 = 2
- Conditionals: if(item.cursed) (4), if(enchantment !== undefined) (4), && (4) = 3 x 4 = 12
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: let surcharge = 0 (6), surcharge += base*0.5 (6), surcharge += base*0.3 (6) = 3 x 6 = 18

itemSurcharge mass: 5 + 3 + 2 + 12 + 0 + 18 = **40**

**`calculateBasePremium` function mass:**
- Constants: 0, 0, 1, 0 = 4 x 1 = 4
- Bindings: items (1), groups (1), totalSurcharge (1), item (1), type (1), count (1), total (1) = 7 x 1 = 7
- Invocations: new Map (2), groups.set (2), groups.get (2), itemSurcharge (2), COMPONENT_TYPES.has (2), BASE_PREMIUM[type] (2) = 6 x 2 = 12
- Conditionals: if(COMPONENT_TYPES.has && count===BLOCK_SIZE) (4), && (4) = 2 x 4 = 8
- Loops: for(item of items) (5), for([type,count] of groups) (5) = 2 x 5 = 10
- Assignments: let totalSurcharge = 0 (6), totalSurcharge += (6), let total = 0 (6), total += BLOCK_PREMIUM (6), total += count*BASE_PREMIUM (6) = 5 x 6 = 30

calculateBasePremium mass: 4 + 7 + 12 + 8 + 10 + 30 = **71**

**Combined Total Mass: 40 + 71 = 111**

Mass Change: 102 -> 111 (delta +9)

Mass increased slightly, but this is justified by Rule 2 (Reveals Intent). The benefits are:

1. **Separation of concerns**: `calculateBasePremium` now has a clean loop body -- grouping and surcharge accumulation are clearly separated.
2. **Named concept**: `itemSurcharge` names the concept of "per-item surcharge calculation" explicitly. Future surcharge types (there's a todo for stacking cursed + enchantment) will naturally extend this single function.
3. **Reduced cognitive load in main function**: The main function went from having 3 mutable accumulators and 2 conditional blocks inside a loop to 1 mutable accumulator and 1 function call.
4. **Single Responsibility**: Each function now does one thing -- `itemSurcharge` computes surcharges for one item, `calculateBasePremium` orchestrates grouping + block discount + total.
5. **`base` binding**: The local `const base = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` avoids repeating the lookup and names what the value represents.

Rule 2 explicitly trumps APP mass, so this is the right trade-off.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `itemSurcharge(item: Item): number` helper from inline surcharge logic
- Collapsed two separate surcharge accumulators (`cursedSurcharge`, `enchantmentSurcharge`) into single `totalSurcharge` in main function
- Introduced `base` local binding in `itemSurcharge` to name the base premium lookup

**Benefits**:
- Main function loop body reduced from 3 accumulators + 2 conditionals to 1 accumulator + 1 function call
- Surcharge logic is now isolated, testable, and extensible
- Named concept (`itemSurcharge`) makes the code self-documenting

**Mass**: 102 -> 111 (+9, justified by Rule 2 clarity gain)
**Tests**: All 13 passing

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `itemSurcharge` helper to separate per-item surcharge calculation from base premium grouping logic
- **Mass Change**: 102 -> 111 (+9, justified by Rule 2: Reveals Intent)
- **Tests**: All 13 passing

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

input.new_string:
- Naming established: `cursedSurcharge` (accumulator for item-level cursed modifier, kept separate from base premium total)

## After cycle 11
- Change: extracted `itemSurcharge(item: Item): number` helper; collapsed `cursedSurcharge`/`enchantmentSurcharge` into single `totalSurcharge` accumulator in main function
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- separates per-item surcharge logic from grouping/block-discount logic; names the concept; simplifies main loop body from 3 accumulators + 2 conditionals to 1 accumulator + 1 call
- Naming established: `itemSurcharge` (function computing all item-level surcharges for one item), `totalSurcharge` (accumulator in main function)
